agent_outputs.raw: This benchmark has two serious methodological problems that invalidate the reported AutoML advantage. First, target encoding and imputation were fitted on the full two-year dataset before cross-validation folds were created. Target encoding for high-cardinality fields (repair shop, assessor ID) uses the mean of the target variable within each category. When the encoding is computed on the full dataset, the fold-level evaluation statistics already contain outcome information from the held-out fold — a classic cross-fold leakage mechanism. Second, the operating threshold was selected to achieve 80% recall on the held-out test set. Regardless of whether 80% recall is an operational requirement, selecting the threshold on the test set makes the precision estimate optimistic. Third, the comparison is asymmetric: the AutoML system benefits from this leaky preprocessing and test-set threshold selection, while the baseline retains its original validation-set threshold from a prior, different protocol. A fair comparison requires fold-specific preprocessing and matched threshold-selection procedures for both systems.

The benchmark cannot be accepted. Two separate contamination paths inflate the AutoML result. First: target encoding fitted on the full dataset before fold creation leaks label information into each fold's evaluation. Target encoding is a valid technique when cross-fitted — this is precisely the error it avoids. Second: the final operating threshold was selected to achieve a fixed recall target on the test set. The 80% recall is operationally fixed, but the test set was used to find what threshold achieves it — this makes the precision at that threshold an in-sample estimate, not a held-out estimate. The baseline comparison is further compromised because it was evaluated under a different protocol. All three issues together mean the claimed advantage could be entirely artifactual. A leakage-free rerun with matched protocols is required.

The evaluation methodology has two critical flaws. Preprocessing leakage: fitting target encoding on the full dataset before fold creation allows outcome information from the test portion to leak into the training portion through the encoding. This is the standard error when target encoding is applied globally rather than within each fold. Test-set threshold contamination: even though 80% recall is an operational requirement, using the test set to find the corresponding threshold means the reported precision at that threshold is optimized on test data, not independently evaluated. The comparison to the baseline is also not apples-to-apples because the baseline uses a different threshold selection protocol. The steering committee should not accept this benchmark.

synthesis: All three agents identify the same two primary flaws: full-data preprocessing leakage and test-set threshold contamination. All three also note the asymmetric protocol as a third issue. All three conclude critique wins and the benchmark should not be accepted. The convergence is unanimous.

etd_constraint.condition: Rebuild AutoML pipeline with fold-specific target encoding; use validation set for threshold selection; re-evaluate both AutoML and baseline under identical protocol on an independent test set
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: AutoML lift at 80% recall shrinks below 1 percentage point or reverses under leakage-free protocol
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: AutoML still shows at least 0.02 ROC-AUC improvement and 3 percentage point precision gain at 80% recall under leakage-free matched protocol
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: ROC-AUC gain is preserved but threshold-based precision gain does not replicate

all_issues_raised: Full-data target encoding before fold creation (all three agents)
Test-set threshold selection contaminates precision estimate (all three agents)
Asymmetric protocol between AutoML and baseline (all three agents)
